unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Get ready to move! In this unit you will learn about the parts of your body and lots of action words, like hop, jump, clap and wave. You will follow movement instructions and give your own. You will also learn how to stay healthy and strong — by moving every day, eating good food, drinking water and sleeping well. By the end, you will be able to talk about what you are doing right now, just like the children in this unit's story, “The Big Race”.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher audiovisual lecture and learn a few vocabulary words each day.
Complete all six grammar practices, moving from guided examples to independent use.
Listen to the Unit readings, including The Big Race, and answer the comprehension questions.
Complete all six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question quiz and student self-assessment.

readings.title: A Poem: “Reach for the Sky!”
readings.passageScript:
Read the poem and do each action as you say it:
Reach for the sky!
Clap your hands, touch your toes.
Turn around. Put your finger on your nose.
Flap your arms, jump up high.
Wiggle your fingers and reach for the sky!

readings.title: Healthy and Strong
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
Walking, running and cycling are all great ways to keep fit and healthy. Moving your body every day helps you stay strong. Eating healthy food gives you energy to play and learn. Drinking water helps your body work well. Getting enough sleep helps you grow.

readings.title: “Get Up and Move” Day
readings.passageScript:
Listen to this text about a special day at school. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“Today is ‘Get Up and Move’ day at our school. All the children are doing different activities. Some children are jumping and hopping. Others are waving their hands and nodding their heads. Everyone is having fun while moving their bodies. Moving helps us stay healthy and strong.”

readings.title: The Big Race
readings.genre: Realistic school story with lots of dialogue.
readings.theme: A school sports day — moving your body, doing your best, and helping your team.
readings.setting: A primary school field in a Somali town.
readings.passageScript:
It was sports day at the school in Selma's town. The sun was warm and the field was full of children. Everyone was ready to run, jump and hop.
“Drink your water,” said Teacher Samira. “Move your bodies and do your best. A strong body is a healthy body!”
Selma was in the green team with her friends Samira, Adam and Noah. Ishmael was in the red team. Ishmael could run very fast, and he liked everyone to know it.
“My legs are the fastest legs in the school,” said Ishmael. He waved his arms and jumped up high. “The red team will win!”
“Do not worry,” Samira said to her team. She nodded her head. “We will run together and do our best.”
The first race was the hopping race. The children lined up. Teacher Samira raised her hand.
“Ready, steady, go!” she called.
The children hopped on one foot across the field. Noah was hopping fast, but he wobbled and nearly fell. “Keep going, Noah!” shouted Selma, clapping her hands. Noah did not give up. He hopped all the way to the line.
Next came the big relay race. In a relay, each runner runs and then passes a stick to the next runner. Selma was the last runner for the green team.
“Ready, steady, go!” called Teacher Samira again.
Samira ran fast. Adam ran faster. Then Noah passed the stick to Selma. But Selma's tummy felt full of butterflies. Her legs felt slow.
“I am running as fast as I can,” Selma puffed, “but Ishmael is winning!”
Isaac was far in front. He was so busy waving to the crowd that he did not look at his feet. Suddenly he tripped and fell onto the grass. His stick rolled away.
Selma ran past him. Her team was cheering. “Go, Selma, go!” they shouted, clapping and jumping. Selma reached the line first. The green team had won!
Everyone cheered and waved. But Selma looked back. Ishmael was still sitting on the grass. He was rubbing his knee, and he looked very sad.
Selma did not jump for joy. She walked back to Ishmael and put out her hand.
“Are you hurt?” she asked kindly.
“Only a little,” said Ishmael. “But I lost. And I said so many silly things.”
Selma smiled. “You are still the fastest runner in the school,” she said. “Come on. Stand up.”
She helped Ishmael stand. Together they walked back, and all the children clapped for both of them.
Teacher Samira nodded and smiled. “Well done, everyone,” she said. “Selma, you ran your best and you never gave up. And you were kind to Ishmael. That is the true winner.”
That evening, Selma told her mother about the big race. “I won,” she said, “but the best part was helping Ishmael. Next time, we will be on the same team!”
Her mother hugged her. “You have a fast body and a kind heart,” she said. “Now drink your water and rest. Tomorrow is another day to move and play.”

comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Turn around. Put your finger on your nose. Flap your arms, jump up high. Wiggle your fingers and reach for the sky!
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Turn around. Put your finger on your nose. Flap your arms, jump up high. Wiggle your fingers and reach for the sky!

grammar.title: Giving Commands (Imperatives)
grammar.explanation: A command tells someone what to do. We use commands to give movement instructions, like in a game or an exercise. This helps you lead an action game and follow your teacher.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
A command starts with the verb. You do not need to say “you” at the start.
Touch your toes.
Clap your hands.
Jump up high.
Stand on one foot.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You touching your toes.”  ✗
Fix: Start with the plain verb: “Touch your toes.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: A command is short and bossy! It jumps straight to the action word: “Jump!” “Clap!” “Stand!”
grammar.practice:
Write the command for each picture. Use a verb + a body part.
______ your ______.  (someone touching their toes)
______ your ______.  (someone clapping their hands)
______ up ______.    (someone jumping high)
______ on one ______. (someone standing on one foot)

grammar.title: Giving Commands (Imperatives): Use It
grammar.explanation: A command tells someone what to do. We use commands to give movement instructions, like in a game or an exercise. This helps you lead an action game and follow your teacher. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 3 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
A command starts with the verb. You do not need to say “you” at the start.
Touch your toes.
Clap your hands.
Jump up high.
Stand on one foot.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You touching your toes.”  ✗
Fix: Start with the plain verb: “Touch your toes.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: A command is short and bossy! It jumps straight to the action word: “Jump!” “Clap!” “Stand!”
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: Actions Happening Now (Present Continuous)
grammar.explanation: We use the present continuous to talk about an action that is happening right now. It helps you say what you and other people are doing at this moment.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use: subject + am / is / are + verb+ing.
Use am with I:  “I am jumping.”
Use is with he, she, it:  “She is hopping.”  “He is clapping.”
Use are with you, we, they:  “They are waving.”  “We are nodding.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “She jumping.”  ✗    Fix: “She is jumping.”  ✓  (Do not forget the helping word am / is / are.)
grammar.memoryTip: Say the little rhyme: “I am, he/she/it is, you/we/they are — don't forget the -ing!”
grammar.practice:
Choose am, is, or are:
I ____ clapping my hands.
He ____ touching his toes.
They ____ waving their arms.
We ____ hopping on one foot.
She ____ nodding her head.

grammar.title: Actions Happening Now (Present Continuous): Use It
grammar.explanation: We use the present continuous to talk about an action that is happening right now. It helps you say what you and other people are doing at this moment. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 3 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use: subject + am / is / are + verb+ing.
Use am with I:  “I am jumping.”
Use is with he, she, it:  “She is hopping.”  “He is clapping.”
Use are with you, we, they:  “They are waving.”  “We are nodding.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “She jumping.”  ✗    Fix: “She is jumping.”  ✓  (Do not forget the helping word am / is / are.)
grammar.memoryTip: Say the little rhyme: “I am, he/she/it is, you/we/they are — don't forget the -ing!”
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: Making the -ing Form
grammar.explanation: To say an action is happening now, we add -ing to the verb. Most verbs are easy, but some verbs change their spelling. Getting this right helps your writing look neat and correct.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Most verbs: just add -ing.  jump → jumping,  clap? (see below),  wave? (see below)
Verbs ending in a silent e: drop the e, then add -ing.  wave → waving,  wiggle → wiggling.
Short verbs ending in one vowel + one consonant: double the last letter, then add -ing.  hop → hopping,  run → running.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She is waveing.”  ✗    Fix: Drop the silent e first: “She is waving.”  ✓
Mistake: “He is hoping on one foot.”  ✗    Fix: Double the p: “He is hopping.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Silent e says “bye-bye” before -ing (wave → waving). A short word with one vowel doubles its last letter for a friend (hop → hopping).
grammar.practice:
Write the -ing form of each verb:
wave → __________
hop → __________
clap → __________
wiggle → __________
touch → __________

grammar.title: Making the -ing Form: Use It
grammar.explanation: To say an action is happening now, we add -ing to the verb. Most verbs are easy, but some verbs change their spelling. Getting this right helps your writing look neat and correct. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 3 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Most verbs: just add -ing.  jump → jumping,  clap? (see below),  wave? (see below)
Verbs ending in a silent e: drop the e, then add -ing.  wave → waving,  wiggle → wiggling.
Short verbs ending in one vowel + one consonant: double the last letter, then add -ing.  hop → hopping,  run → running.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She is waveing.”  ✗    Fix: Drop the silent e first: “She is waving.”  ✓
Mistake: “He is hoping on one foot.”  ✗    Fix: Double the p: “He is hopping.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Silent e says “bye-bye” before -ing (wave → waving). A short word with one vowel doubles its last letter for a friend (hop → hopping).
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Naming Body Parts
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Look at yourself in a mirror, or point to your own body. Say:
“This is my ______.”  (head, arm, hand, tummy)
“These are my ______.”  (fingers, toes)
Record yourself naming at least 10 body parts. Check: Did you say them clearly? Did you use “This is my” for one part and “These are my” for more than one?

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Describing Actions
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Do an action, then say what you are doing right now:
“I am ______.”  (jumping, clapping, waving)
“He / She is ______.”
“They are ______.”

speaking.title: Speaking Game — Simon Says
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family. This is a movement command game:
One person gives a command: “Simon says, touch your toes!”
Only do the action if they say “Simon says” first.
If they just say “Jump!” without “Simon says”, stay still. Then swap turns.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Movement Coach
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Give six clear movement commands. Your partner follows them, then swaps roles with you.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Use clear command verbs and a safe pace.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Healthy Habit Interview
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Ask and answer six questions about moving, food, water and sleep.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Answer in complete sentences and give one reason.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - Sports Reporter
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Describe what six people are doing now in a race or exercise scene.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Use am, is or are with an action ending in -ing.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 1 — Label Your Body Parts
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Draw a simple person. Label at least 10 body parts. Then write a sentence about each one:
“I use my ______ to ______.”  (for example: “I use my hands to clap.”)
writing.modelText:
Labels: head, eyes, ears, nose, mouth, arms, hands, fingers, legs, feet.
My eyes help me see. My legs help me run.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Describing Actions
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write five sentences about what people are doing now. Use am / is / are + verb+ing:
“The boy is jumping up and down.”
“The girl is waving her hand.”
“The children are clapping their hands.”
writing.modelText: I move every day to keep my body strong. I can clap my hands, touch my toes and jump. I also drink water and rest.
writing.sentenceStarter: I move every day to keep my body strong.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 3 — My Healthy Habits List
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a list of at least five healthy habits. Draw a small picture for each one:
“I drink water every day.”
“I eat fruits and vegetables.”
“I play outside and move my body.”
writing.modelText:
1. Drink clean water.
2. Eat healthy food.
3. Move my body.
4. Wash my hands.
5. Sleep and rest.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Story response: The Big Race
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three sentences about a game or sport you like. Use action words, like: “I am running.” “I am jumping.”
Imagine your friend loses a race and feels sad. Write two kind things you could say to them.
Draw Selma helping Ishmael stand up. Write one sentence under your picture.
writing.modelText: Ishmael wanted to win the race, but he learned to be fair and kind. Selma checked that he was safe. I learned that good sportsmanship matters more than winning.
writing.sentenceStarter: Ishmael wanted to win the race, but he learned to be fair and kind.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 4 - My Exercise Routine
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six ordered instructions for a short, safe exercise routine.
writing.modelText: First, I stretch my arms. Next, I hop five times. Then, I drink water.
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 5 - A Healthy Day
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write five sentences explaining how you move, eat, drink and sleep to stay healthy.
writing.modelText: I move every day. I eat healthy food and drink clean water. I sleep early so my body can rest.
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

activities.title: Activity 1 — Match the Body Part to an Action
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each body part with an action you can do with it. Draw a line, or write the pairs.
Body parts: hands · head · arms · fingers · toes · legs
Actions: clap · nod · wave · wiggle · tap · jump
activities.answerSummary: hands → clap  ·  head → nod  ·  arms → wave  ·  fingers → wiggle  ·  toes → tap  ·  legs → jump

activities.title: Activity 2 — Fill in the Blanks (Body Parts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the complete sentence and fill in the correct body part:
I use my ______ to see.
I use my ______ to hear.
I use my ______ to walk.
I use my ______ to hold things.
I use my ______ to smell.
activities.answerSummary: 1. eyes  2. ears  3. legs / feet  4. hands  5. nose

activities.title: Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks (Action Words)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the correct -ing form of the verb in brackets:
I am ______ (jump) up and down.
She is ______ (clap) her hands.
He is ______ (touch) his toes.
They are ______ (wave) their arms.
We are ______ (hop) on one foot.

activities.title: Activity 4 — True or False (Healthy Habits)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
Drinking water helps keep your body healthy.
Eating only sweets gives you energy to learn.
Moving your body every day helps you stay strong.
Watching TV all day is good exercise.
activities.answerSummary: 1. True  2. False (fruits and vegetables give you energy, not only sweets)  3. True  4. False (active play and exercise are good for your body, not watching TV all day)

activities.title: Activity 5 - Build a Movement Sequence
activities.instructionsAndItems: Put six action cards in a safe order, perform the routine, and explain each command.
activities.answerSummary: Answers vary. Require complete, relevant work that follows every instruction and uses the target Unit language accurately.
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

activities.title: Activity 6 - Healthy Choice Sort
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve choices into Helps Me Stay Healthy and Does Not Help Me Stay Healthy.
activities.answerSummary: Answers vary. Require complete, relevant work that follows every instruction and uses the target Unit language accurately.
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

assignments.title: Ready, Steady, Go! learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Complete 'Writing 1 — Label Your Body Parts' and record 'Speaking 1 — Naming Body Parts'. Submit both pieces after checking the writing and speaking rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'head' mean?
quizzes.options: The top part of your body, with your eyes, ears, nose and mouth. | The part from your shoulder to your hand. | The part at the end of your arm, with fingers. | One of the five long parts at the end of your hand.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The top part of your body, with your eyes, ears, nose and mouth.
quizzes.explanation: I wear a hat on my head when it is sunny.

quizzes.question: Which word means: The part from your shoulder to your hand.
quizzes.explanation: I use my arms to carry my school bag.

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'hand'?
quizzes.explanation: I wash my hands before I eat.

quizzes.question: I hold my pencil with my ____s.
quizzes.explanation: I hold my pencil with my fingers.

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.explanation: My tummy feels full after lunch.

quizzes.question: Which word means: One of the five small parts at the end of your foot.
quizzes.explanation: toe: One of the five small parts at the end of your foot. I can wiggle my toes inside my shoes.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which word means: To move your hand from side to side to say hello or goodbye.
quizzes.explanation: wave: To move your hand from side to side to say hello or goodbye. I wave goodbye to my friend.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'hop'?
quizzes.explanation: hop: To jump on one foot. The children hop like little rabbits.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: We ____ over the puddles when it rains.
quizzes.explanation: jump: To push yourself off the ground with both feet. We jump over the puddles when it rains.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.explanation: clap: To hit your hands together to make a sound. We clap our hands after the song.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

liveSessions.title: Ready, Steady, Go!: vocabulary and speaking
liveSessions.beforeSession: Watch the teacher lecture and learn the first vocabulary group.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome; 10 min vocabulary retrieval; 10 min picture talk; 15 min Speaking practice; 5 min reflection

liveSessions.title: Shared reading: The Big Race
liveSessions.beforeSession: Listen to The Big Race and bring one question.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min prediction; 10 min teacher read-aloud; 10 min echo reading; 15 min comprehension; 5 min exit ticket
liveSessions.afterSession: Improve two comprehension answers using text evidence.

liveSessions.title: Grammar workshop: Giving Commands (Imperatives)
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min retrieval; 10 min teacher model; 10 min guided examples; 15 min partner application; 5 min feedback
liveSessions.afterSession: Correct one grammar response and read it aloud.

liveSessions.title: Grammar and speaking: Actions Happening Now (Present Continuous)

liveSessions.afterSession: Finish one independent grammar and speaking task.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

liveSessions.title: Ready, Steady, Go!: guided writing
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring a draft of one Unit writing task.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min model review; 10 min oral rehearsal; 15 min guided writing; 10 min feedback; 5 min revision goal
liveSessions.afterSession: Revise and save one writing piece.

liveSessions.title: Ready, Steady, Go!: review and showcase
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete the ten-question quiz and choose one portfolio piece.

teacherNotes.note:
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the poem with me and help me do the actions?” “What does this word mean: ______?” “Can you play a ‘Get Up and Move’ listening game with me?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you help me practise naming body parts?” “Am I using ‘I am …ing’ correctly?” “Can you play ‘Simon Says’ with me?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check if my action sentences are correct?” “How do I spell this body part: ______?” “What other healthy habits can I add to my list?”
Story: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?” “Can you check my sentences about the game I like?” “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?”
Grammar: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check my grammar answers?” “Why do we say ‘hopping’ with two p's but ‘jumping’ with one?” “Can you give me five more sentences to practise am, is and are?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you help me learn more body part words?” “How do I say this word correctly: ______?” “What is the difference between a finger and a toe?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you help me learn more action words?” “What is the difference between hop and jump?” “Can you say an action for me to do?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Why is drinking water important?” “What are other ways to stay healthy and strong?” “Can you help me write a sentence with the word ‘energy’?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you give me a command to follow?” “Can you check if my command sentence is correct?”

Term placement approved. Outcome mappings, comprehension guidance, vocabulary enrichment, assessments, live sessions, rubrics, writing specifications and answer-key coverage completed in the approved reference model.
teacherNotes.sourceFile: Ehel English Content Template v1 migration

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: hands → clap  ·  head → nod  ·  arms → wave  ·  fingers → wiggle  ·  toes → tap  ·  legs → jump

Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Her legs felt slow. But Selma's tummy felt full of butterflies.

Correct answer: Noun. I wash my hands before I eat.
Correct answer: finger. I hold my pencil with my fingers.
Correct answer: tummy. My tummy feels full after lunch.
